planting.note: Plant small bulbs 3 inches deep in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gritty soil in full sun to part shade; Cantabrian daffodil is the white equivalent of the yellow hoop petticoat (N. bulbocodium), flowering in late winter and early spring. Like N. bulbocodium it naturalizes in thin turf and well-drained rocky grassland. Requires summer dry dormancy — suits Mediterranean-climate gardens or well-drained rockeries that do not receive summer irrigation.

directions: Plant small bulbs in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, gritty soil in part shade; Cantabrian daffodil is the white-flowered hoop petticoat species — the smaller, earlier, purer-white counterpart to the familiar yellow N. bulbocodium — producing the same distinctive flower form with an enormous, widely flared funnel-trumpet dominating tiny, vestigial, thread-like outer petals, but in pure snow-white rather than yellow, flowering even earlier (late winter to very early spring, often before N. bulbocodium). Native to the limestone hills, rocky pastures, and open scrubland of the Atlas Mountains of Morocco and the mountains of southwestern Spain and Portugal, it belongs to the drier, more alkaline end of the hoop petticoat daffodil group's habitat range. The little white funnel-flowers, appearing on 4–6 inch stems in February–March, are among the earliest and purest-white of all bulb flowers. Like all hoop petticoat daffodils it naturalizes freely in thin, well-drained grassland and open rocky soils, building colonies over years. A gentle, early-spring treasure for the rock garden, gravel planting, or naturalistic wild area in zones 7–10.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; mountain allium is a compact, early-blooming ornamental allium ideal for rock gardens and border fronts. It blooms in late spring (May–June), earlier than the large hybrid alliums, with open, lax umbels of bright, deep-rose-pink star-shaped flowers. It increases by offsets and seeds to form small clumps. Mild garlic scent when foliage is crushed.

directions: Plant small bulbs in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; mountain allium is a compact, low-growing allium from the mountain meadows of Central Asia — Turkey, the Caucasus, and Central Asia — that offers one of the most intense, saturated pink-rose flower colors in the ornamental allium range on a plant small enough for the rock garden, border front, or naturalizing in thin, stony turf. The flowers are borne in late spring in rather open, lax, flat-headed umbels of 6–20 individual, six-petaled, starry flowers in a clear, deep magenta-rose to carmine-pink, the petals with a slightly darker central midrib, creating small open umbels of vivid, concentrated pink color on 8–12 inch stems above two narrow, channeled, grass-like leaves. Unlike the large hybrid alliums, the flower head is open and non-spherical, giving it a more informal, naturalistic appearance suited to wild-flower style or rock garden plantings. It blooms before the tall hybrid alliums, filling the late spring bulb gap between the main daffodil-tulip season and the early summer perennial season. Outstanding in combination with Allium hollandicum and later alliums for an extended ornamental allium succession.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4–5 inches deep in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gritty soil in full sun; flax-leaf tulip is a Central Asian species from the arid mountains of Tajikistan and Uzbekistan that requires hot, dry summer dormancy to persist reliably. It naturalizes in lean, very well-drained, sunny conditions where summer is dry, but rots in wet summers. The narrow, gray-green, wavy-edged leaves are distinctive; the brilliant scarlet-red flowers with a small black-and-yellow center basal blotch are among the most vivid of any wild tulip.

directions: Plant bulbs in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; flax-leaf tulip is among the most intensely, brilliantly colored of all wild tulip species — producing in mid-spring (April–May) small but spectacularly vivid, glossy, signal-red to brilliant scarlet-vermillion, bowl-shaped flowers with a distinctive small, sharp-edged black basal blotch edged with yellow at the center of each petal, creating a sparkling tricolor of scarlet, black, and yellow within each flower. The narrow, wavy-edged, gray-green leaves (giving the species epithet 'linifolia' = 'flax-leaved') are held close to the ground in an attractive, somewhat sprawling rosette around the short stem. Native to the rocky, arid, mountain steppes of Central Asia — Tajikistan, Uzbekistan, and adjacent regions — it evolved for hot, dry summers and is one of the better naturalizing wild tulips in dry, lean, sunny garden conditions, returning reliably year after year where summer drainage is good. Combined with T. tarda (yellow), T. praestans (orange-red), or T. clusiana (pink-and-white) in a wild tulip rock garden, it provides the most brilliant red in the palette. A parent of some compact hybrid tulips.

planting.note: Plant tubers 3–4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; lords and ladies grows in the cool, moist months (autumn through spring) and is dormant in summer. The glossy, dark-green, arrow-shaped leaves (often spotted with dark purple-brown blotches, though unspotted forms exist) emerge in autumn and are present through winter and spring — providing attractive winter-green foliage in the shade garden. All parts are highly toxic — especially the attractive orange-red berries. Keep away from children.

directions: Plant tubers in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; lords and ladies is the classic British and European woodland arum — one of the most familiar and architecturally interesting plants of the deciduous forest floor, producing in early spring a distinctive, pale green to whitish-cream spathe (the outer hood) surrounding a dark maroon-purple, club-like spadix (the 'lords') — the whole structure exuding a brief period of heat and a faint odor to attract small insects for pollination. The broad, glossy, dark-green, arrow-shaped leaves (frequently spotted with dark purple-brown blotches) emerge in autumn and persist as an attractive winter-green groundcover through the cold months, disappearing in summer dormancy. In late summer the flowering stem, now leafless, bears a dense cluster of brilliant, traffic-light-orange berries in a tight cylinder on the persistent spadix — one of the most startling and visually striking berry displays of any woodland plant, the vivid orange-red contrasting brilliantly against the dark woodland floor. Self-seeds freely in moist, shaded garden soil to build naturalizing colonies. All parts are highly toxic if ingested — especially the attractive berries, which resemble no edible fruit. A classic plant for a woodland garden, shaded wall planting, or naturalistic woodland-edge garden in zones 5–9.

subcategory: shade woodland novelty arum with mouse-tail spathe

planting.note: Plant small rhizomes or clumps 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; mouse plant spreads by rhizome to form dense groundcover colonies in moist, shaded conditions. It is a cool-season grower — emerging and flowering in early spring, going dormant in summer heat. The common name refers to the tiny, chocolate-and-white spathe-tipped flowers which, viewed from above, resemble small mice diving under the leaves.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; mouse plant is a small, shade-loving, Mediterranean arum relative from the moist, shaded woodlands of Spain and Italy that is one of the most charming and conversation-stopping novelty plants in the woodland garden — producing in early spring, hidden among the glossy, bright-green, arrow-shaped leaves, tiny, peculiar, bicolor flowers: each one a small, tapering spathe that is dark, shining chocolate-brown at the base (the 'body') curving up through a white-and-dark transition into a long, slender, thread-like tail — the 'mouse tail' — up to 4 inches long. The flowers are so cleverly concealed that they appear to be small mice diving nose-first under the leaf canopy, with only the long tail visible above the foliage. The common name 'mouse plant' perfectly captures the instantly recognizable illusion. In a moss-covered, shaded corner of a woodland garden, with a colony of these plants apparently populated by dozens of diving mice tails sticking up among the fresh, glossy leaves, it is an unfailing delight and conversation piece. It spreads steadily by rhizome in favorable moist, shaded conditions to form a groundcover colony. All parts are mildly toxic. An essential plant for the woodland curiosity garden.
